sdo:itemDesc.dc:title: Handwritten letter from Paul Wittgenstein to Schenker, dated “Tuesday” [November 5 or 12, 1912?]
sdo:itemDesc.dc:description: Paul Wittgenstein invites Schenker to examine the autograph manuscript of Beethoven Op. 109.

tei:opener.tei:salute: Sehr geehrter Herr!Receipt of this letter is not recorded in Schenker’s diary. The suggested editorial datings are based on Schenker’s diary entry for November 12, 1912: “With Dr. Mandyczewski at Wittgenstein's, an examination of the autograph of Op. 109. Quite unexpectedly surprising discoveries, which confirm everything I have taught my pupils in spite of the corruption of the text. Nottebohm's and Mandyczewski's notes prove to be too superficial, and do not come close to the heart of the matter. § Dr. Mandyczewski is concerned about leaving me on my own for a while with Mr. Paul Wittgenstein! He was not keen to see the young gentleman becoming better acquainted with me. He suggested that I depart with him and return the following Tuesday, and he was himself on his way out when Mr. Wittgenstein said that he, too, was prepared to join him. And so I was able to study the autograph for another hour, on my own and undisturbed.”.

tei:div.tei:p:
Die betreffenden Manuscripte stehen ganz zu Ihrer Verfügung. Sie brauchen bloß zu telefonieren oder zu schreiben, wann Sie kommen, damit das opus 109 herausgeräumt wird. Wenn esi.e. the inspection of Op. 109. auf länger als
Sie glauben dauert, macht das gar nichts.

tei:postscript.tei:p: Nachträglich noch meinen verbindlichsten Dank für Ihr interessantes Buch!Probably Schenker’s monograph Beethovens neunte Sinfonie, published in July 1912. Der kleine Korngold kommt schlecht weg dabei!Erich Wolfgang Korngold (1897–1957), who had been pronounced a “genius” by Gustav Mahler in 1907, had just had his first orchestral works performed to great acclaim in 1912. It is possible that this remark was prompted not by the content of the book but by the dedication: “Dem Andenken des letzten Meisters Deutscher Tonkunst Johannes Brahms” (“To the memory of the last master of German Composition, Johannes Brahms”).

tei:div.tei:p:
The manuscripts in question are entirely at your disposal. You need only telephone or write stating when you will be coming, and the autograph manuscript of Op. 109 will be got out of storage. If it
takes longer than you expect, that will not be a problem.

tei:postscript.tei:p: Additionally, my heartfelt thanks for your interesting book! The little Korngold comes off badly by comparison!
